coredata.dc:title: DFT, real sample and smartphone studies of fluorescent probe, N-doped carbon quantum dots for sensitive and selective detection of bilirubin
coredata.dc:creator: Kumar, Himanshu
Duhan, Jyoti
Obrai, Sangeeta
coredata.dc:description: In this investigation, nitrogen-doped carbon quantum dots (NCQDs) were successfully synthesized, showcasing robust fluorescence characteristics and photostability. These NCQDs demonstrated biocompatibility and were utilized as effective sensing agents for bilirubin (BR) detection. Their remarkable Stern-Volmer constant, attributed to exceptional molar absorptivity and photoluminescence quantum yield, rendered them highly proficient in BR sensing. Various analytical techniques such as X-ray diffraction (XRD), energy-dispersive X-ray spectroscopy (EDS), transmission electron microscopy (TEM), scanning electron microscopy (SEM), atomic force microscopy (AFM), and X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS) were employed to characterize the synthesized NCQDs, providing comprehensive insights into their structural, elemental, chemical composition, particle size, morphology, and optical properties. For sensing purposes, UV–Vis, steady-state, and time-resolved fluorescence spectroscopic techniques were utilized. The average particle size of the NCQDs was determined to be approximately 4.5 ± 0.3 nm. Notably, the Stern-Volmer constant (Ksv) for BR sensing from these quantum dots surpassed previously reported values in the literature. A linear relationship between emission intensity ratio and BR concentration within a range of 1.35–14.20 μM was observed. Accurate quantification of BR was achieved with a high Stern-Volmer constant (Ksv) S11 of approximately 7.48 × 105 M-1 and a limit of detection (LOD) of 4.86 μM. Leveraging the NCQDs as fluorescent probes, a highly sensitive and selective BR detection method based on the Förster resonance energy transfer (FRET) mechanism was established, with emission occurring at 360 nm. Density functional theory (DFT) studies supported FRET as the primary mechanism for fluorescence quenching, indicating electron transfer from NCQDs to BR, as evidenced by the energies of the lowest unoccupied molecular orbitals (LUMOs) of both NCQDs and BR. Real sample analyses of BR levels in human serum yielded a recovery range of 94.8 % to 104.21 %, validating the practical applicability of the method. Furthermore, smartphone-based BR detection using NCQDs achieved an LOD of 11.74 µM.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Nitrogen-doped carbon quantum dots (N-CQD)
Fluorescence resonance energy transfer (FRET)
Density functional theory (DFT)
Colorimetric
Human serum
Smartphone

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Detection of bilirubin on colorimetric test papers
Detection of BR in human blood serum

xocs:funding.xocs:funding-agency-matched-string: Ministry of Human Resource and Development

xocs:funding-list.xocs:funding-text: The authors express their sincere gratitude to the Ministry of Human Resource and Development (MHRD), New Delhi, for awarding a research assistantship to Mr. Himanshu Kumar. They also acknowledge Dr. B.R. Ambedkar National Institute of Technology, Jalandhar, for providing the necessary research infrastructure.

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: NCQDs in visible and UV light.
(a) TEM image (b) The average particle sizes of quantum dots (c) XRD spectra of NCQDs (d) AFM image of NCQDs.
(a) XPS spectra of NCQDs (B) C1s high-resolution XPS spectra (c) The high-resolution XPS spectra of N1s (d) The high-resolution XPS spectra of S2p (e) The high-resolution XPS spectra of NCQDs.
(a) UV–Vis absorbance and Fluorescence spectrum of NCQDs and (b) Spectral overlap between the normalized absorbance spectrum of BR and normalized fluorescence spectrum of NCQDs.
(a) Fluorescence intensity of NCQDs with aq sodium chloride solution. (b) Fluorescence spectra of NCQDs at room temperature with time(sec) (c) Time-dependent fluorescence response of NCQDs.
(a) Fluorescence Quenching of NCQDs with successive addition of BR and (b) Stern–Volmer plot of (I0/I) - 1 v/s concentration of BR (c) CIE chromaticity diagram for BR analysis.
(a) UV–Vis absorbance spectra of NCQDs with successive addition of BR and (b) Histogram of fluorescence quenching efficiency of NCQDs towards BR and other analogous analytes.
Fluorescence Lifetime decay profile of the NCQDSS with and without BL (λex = 360 nm, λem = 450 nm).
Frontier Molecular Orbitals (HOMO-LUMO) of NCQDs and BR.
Recovery percentage of BR spiked serum.
Smartphone-based determination of BL ions by NCQDs in H2O.
Comparative performance previously reported Bilirubin sensor.

row.entry: Types of sensors for Melatonin reported
DFT, Real Sample And Smartphone Studies of Fluorescent probe, N-doped Carbon Quantum Dots for sensitive and selective detection of Bilirubin

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: Structural parameters of the optimized donor, acceptor and complex.
Mulliken atomic charges for some centers of donor, acceptor and complex.

head.ce:title: DFT, real sample and smartphone studies of fluorescent probe, N-doped carbon quantum dots for sensitive and selective detection of bilirubin

ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Department of Chemistry, Dr. B R Ambedkar national Institute of Technology, Jalandhar, Punjab 144008, India

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Dr. B R Ambedkar national Institute of Technology

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Department of Chemistry, Dr. B R Ambedkar national Institute of Technology, Jalandhar, Punjab 144008, India

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: In this investigation, nitrogen-doped carbon quantum dots (NCQDs) were successfully synthesized, showcasing robust fluorescence characteristics and photostability. These NCQDs demonstrated biocompatibility and were utilized as effective sensing agents for bilirubin (BR) detection. Their remarkable Stern-Volmer constant, attributed to exceptional molar absorptivity and photoluminescence quantum yield, rendered them highly proficient in BR sensing. Various analytical techniques such as X-ray diffraction (XRD), energy-dispersive X-ray spectroscopy (EDS), transmission electron microscopy (TEM), scanning electron microscopy (SEM), atomic force microscopy (AFM), and X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS) were employed to characterize the synthesized NCQDs, providing comprehensive insights into their structural, elemental, chemical composition, particle size, morphology, and optical properties. For sensing purposes, UV–Vis, steady-state, and time-resolved fluorescence spectroscopic techniques were utilized. The average particle size of the NCQDs was determined to be approximately 4.5 ± 0.3 nm. Notably, the Stern-Volmer constant (Ksv) for BR sensing from these quantum dots surpassed previously reported values in the literature. A linear relationship between emission intensity ratio and BR concentration within a range of 1.35–14.20 μM was observed. Accurate quantification of BR was achieved with a high Stern-Volmer constant (Ksv) S11 of approximately 7.48 × 105 M-1 and a limit of detection (LOD) of 4.86 μM. Leveraging the NCQDs as fluorescent probes, a highly sensitive and selective BR detection method based on the Förster resonance energy transfer (FRET) mechanism was established, with emission occurring at 360 nm. Density functional theory (DFT) studies supported FRET as the primary mechanism for fluorescence quenching, indicating electron transfer from NCQDs to BR, as evidenced by the energies of the lowest unoccupied molecular orbitals (LUMOs) of both NCQDs and BR. Real sample analyses of BR levels in human serum yielded a recovery range of 94.8 % to 104.21 %, validating the practical applicability of the method. Furthermore, smartphone-based BR detection using NCQDs achieved an LOD of 11.74 µM.

ce:list-item.ce:para: The authors do not have permission to share data.

ce:section.ce:para: Bilirubin is the key pigment generated by the breakdown of haemoglobin, myoglobin, peroxidases and cytochromes. Bilirubin exists in conjugated (water soluble) and unconjugated (water insoluble) forms. The total bilirubin concentrations in human serum varies from 0.3 to 1.2 mg/dL. Bilirubin levels may get raised due to liver dysfunctions such as cirrhosis, liver cancer, autoimmune hepatitis, Gilbert's syndrome, cholestasis, bile duct inflammation with common symptoms of jaundice, nausea, vomiting, dark urine, etc., In jaundice, the bilirubin level may increase up to 20 mg/dL [1–3]. Several experimental techniques have been established for the detection of BR like high-performance liquid chromatography HPLC [4–6], electro-chemical [7–13], mass spectrometry [14–16], voltammetry [17,18], chemiluminescence [19], capillary electrophoresis [20], enzymatic assay [21], piezoelectricity [22] and fluorescence [23–48]. Computational studies are beneficial over all these techniques as they do not require complex instrumentation, long duration, or skilled manpower. Moreover, the theoretical study of interactions between molecules paves the way for experimental sensing of biomolecules, toxic chemicals, metal ions, etc. [49]. Few quantum dots have been designed for sensing applications and their quantum chemical characterization is also carried out [50].

In recent years, there has been significant progress in the detection of bilirubin using various techniques such as fluorescence with engineered nanoparticles, nanosheets, metal-organic frameworks, metal nanoclusters, carbon dots, quantum dots, and graphene quantum dots. Among these, NCQDs have gained considerable attention due to their exceptional optical, chemical, electronic, and mechanical properties. These properties make them particularly valuable for applications such as fluorescence imaging and photothermal therapy of oral cancer. Furthermore, the photoluminescence of NCQDs can be easily adjusted by altering their surface and size chemistry NCQDs offer distinct advantages compared to conventional carbon quantum dot sheets, including tenable photoluminescence and slow hot carrier relaxation. These unique characteristics has led to their widespread use in various fields, including sensing (e.g., TNT detection), drug delivery, catalytic reactions, and bioimaging [51–58]. Their suitability for biological applications is attributed to their high stability, low toxicity, and solubility in various solvents. Additionally, a smartphone app named the "RGB color detector" has proven to be a valuable tool for color change detection, aiding in sensing purposes and determining the limit of detection.

In the context of our research, we have successfully synthesized water-soluble NCQDs with blue luminescence. These NCQDs serve as the basis for a label-free fluorescence-based detection method designed to detect bilirubin (BR) with high sensitivity and selectivity. Our investigations have revealed that BR can efficiently quench the fluorescence emitted by the synthesized NCQDs through a mechanism known as fluorescence resonance energy transfer (FRET). This sensing approach is not only cost-effective but also highly sensitive and biocompatible.

Bilirubin was purchased from Sigma Aldrich. Urea, citric acid, glucose, urea, ascorbic acid, and NaOH solutions were purchased from Loba. All chemicals were applied directly without preparation, and distilled water was used as a solution.

UV–VIS absorption spectra were taken with a UV–VIS spectrophotometer (Agilent), and a photoluminescence spectrophotometer with excitation and emission wavelengths of 360 and 450 nm, respectively, using a 300kv accelerating voltage of QDs used Memorandum developed, was used, . EDS for elemental analysis, Scanning electron microscopy (SEM) for morphology, X-ray diffraction (XRD) for angle and X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy were used Binding strength and chemical state. RGB color detector software was used to detect color changes as in the QDs BR. Time-resolved fluorescence spectrometers were used to examine the overall lifetime and study the interaction mechanism.

Deionized water (DI) was prepared using NCQD and BR stock solutions. Fluorescence was measured at an excitation wavelength of 360 nm and an emission wavelength of 450 nm by adding solutions of aqueous BR to the N-CQD assay solution from 0 to 14.20 μM.

Preparation of NCQDs Citric acid was used as carbon precursor and nitric acid was used as nitrogen precursor by furnace assisted method by mixing citric acid (88.3752 mg) and nitric acid (12.602 mg). NCQDs were prepared and heated hours in an muffle furnace at 300 °C. The product was cooled to room temperature to form a brown-colored powder, which was soluble in water and ethanol. The purified compound was dissolved in water and centrifuged at 16,000 rpm for 15 min-CQDs black colored powder dissolves in water and becomes visible light, then the color changes to yellow and then under UV light in the color changes from red to cyan (Fig. 1 ).

The prepared NCQDs were characterized using Transmission electron microscope (TEM), UV–visible and fluorescence spectroscopic methods. The morphology of NCQDs was characterized by TEM analysis shown in (Fig. 2a ). The average particle size was 4.5 ± 0.3 nm. The literature review shows that particles in the plane (002) with an angle of 15.230 (Fig. 2c) show approximately the highest intensities observed in [59–61] AFM images, as shown in Fig. 2d, viz NCQDS surface height is constant 4–5 is less than nm. This observation provides strong evidence supporting the carbon quantum dot-like nature of the structures

The composition and particle size of the synthesized materials were studied using ZEISS Field Emission Scanning Electron Microscope (FE-SEM) and EDS. NCQDs during spherical aggregate morphology. The SEM image(S3) of the NCQDs clearly showed that the size of QD deposited on the NCQDs surface was less than 10 nm (Fig. 3 ). The EDS spectrum proves that nitrogen incorporated into carbon quantum dots with NCQDs elemental composition (C:49.54 %, N:1.39 %, O:49.07 %) is well accompanied.

According to the X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS) scan shown in (Fig. 4a ), the NCQDs were found to be mainly composed of carbon, nitrogen, and oxygen with bond energy maxima of 292.4 eV, 399.2 eV, and 531.8 eV, respectively. . . . . in any case. C1s high-resolution spectrum observed in (Fig. 4b) shows three peaks at 282 eV, 284.1 eV, and 287.2 eV due to C = C, C = O, CO-C as well as N1s high-resolution spectrum due to CN-C and N = O group It shows two peaks at 396.9 eV and 398.4 eV (Fig. 4c). In O1s spectrum, three peaks at 528.8 eV, 529.8 eV, and 532.2 eV are attributed to C = O, COH and CN-C as shown in (Fig. 4d) [62].

The spherical particles that have been seen are monodispersed. The extended band-edge absorption and absorbance maxima peak at 328 nm, which correlate to the CO bond's n–n* transition, have been detected in the UV-visible absorbance spectra of NCQDs (Fig. 5a ). When activated at a wavelength of 360 nm, fluorescence exhibits a broad emission spectrum with a maximum fluorescence intensity at 450 nm (λmax) (Fig. 5a).

The graph in Fig. 6c shows the optimization of incubation time, which shows that NCQDs fluorescence decreases sharply at 450 nm upon incubation in BR until it reaches a plateau at 55 min. The subsequent fluorescence decay occurred slowly for 100 min, followed by a continuous but small change for 120 min when the fluorescence response reached equilibrium At this point, the fluorescence intensity of the sensor device increased greatly reduced. It is evident that the optimal time for the detection of this process is 120 min, as shown in (Fig. 6b). For photostability, the time-dependent fluorescence response showed that the NCQDs solution was exposed to one hour of radiation with only a 2 % decrease in fluorescence intensity (Fig. 6a) in addition to the stability of NCQDs in an aqueous sodium chloride solution (ranging from 0 to 140 µM) was assessed by monitoring fluorescence intensity, which remained constant throughout the experiment (Fig. 6a).

For the sensory performance evaluation of the developed system, different BR rates were analysed under optimal conditions. The blue emitting fluorescence of NCQDs solution is completely quenched after adding the BR, optical image shown in Fig. S8. The fluorescence spectra of NCQDs were recorded after addition of different amounts of BR (Fig. 7a ). As the concentration of BR increased, the fluorescence intensity of NCQDs decreased correspondingly. To evaluate the sensitive performance parameters, a calibration graph was constructed using the fluorescence intensity ratio (I0/I)−1, where I represents the amount of fluorescence intensity of the system at 450 nm with the analyte, and I0 represents the amount of fluorescence intensity free of the reference. This graph, known as the Stern-Volmer plot, can be seen in (Fig. 7b) There was a linear relationship observed with a limit of detection (LOD) of 4.86 μM (S1) in the BR concentration range a range 1.35–14.20 μM. This fluorescence quenching phenomenon was quantified by Stern-Volmer analysis, and the Stern-Volmer constant (KSV) [62] was calculated using the following equation.
I 0
/ I = 1 +
K
S V
[ Q ]

The Stern-Volmer constant, denoted as KSV, serves as a quantitative indicator of the degree of interaction between the fluorophore and the quencher. A higher KSV value signifies a more effective quenching process. Examination of the Stern-Volmer constant enables researchers to delve into the nature of molecular interactions between the fluorophore and the quencher, encompassing both dynamic and static quenching mechanisms. In disciplines like environmental science and biochemistry, the Stern-Volmer equation finds utility in monitoring environmental factors or identifying specific analytes. For example, alterations in fluorescence intensity can signal the presence of pollutants or biomolecules, facilitating swift and sensitive analysis. In the realm of drug discovery and biomedical exploration, investigations employing the Stern-Volmer equation aid in comprehending the binding interactions between potential drug compounds and their target biomolecules, thus assisting in the development of more potent therapeutic agents. Moreover, in the domains of material science and nanotechnology, the Stern-Volmer equation is instrumental in characterizing the attributes of nanomaterials such as quantum dots and nanoparticles. A thorough understanding of the quenching behavior of these materials is imperative for their deployment in sensors, imaging modalities, and optoelectronics. Scientists often leverage the Stern-Volmer constant to gauge the quenching efficiencies across various. This capability proves invaluable in a myriad of scientific and research applications. From the Stern-Volmer plot, estimated KSV values ​​of 7.48 × 105 M − 1 were determined, as shown in Fig. 7b. This large KSV value indicates that NCQD can be widely used to detect highly sensitive BR. The exceptional sensitivity and fast response observed for free BR can be attributed to two possible optical mechanisms: photo-induced electron transfer (PET) and Förster resonance energy transfer (FRET) [63–65]. The high likelihood of Förster Resonance Energy Transfer (FRET) is supported by the hydrogen bonding interactions and significant spectral overlap (J(λ) = 7.90 × 1013 nm4M−1cm−1) between the emission spectra of NCQDs (donor) and the absorbance spectra of BR (acceptor), as depicted in Fig. 6b. Furthermore, there was no observable spectral shift in the UV–Vis and PL titration study, as illustrated in (Fig. 7a) This absence of spectral shift strongly favours the FRET-based sensing mechanism over the PET mechanism. To assess selectivity, we used CIE 1931 chromaticity, as shown in (Fig. 7c), to visually represent the BR titration based on fluorescence spectrum data. The color transition from cyan to a yellowish(S5) area upon the addition of BR was consistent with the observed change in fluorescence color [66].

Then, the absence of any obvious spectral changes in the UV–Vis titration study (Fig. 8a ) is attributed to the selectivity of BR compared to some other analogue analytes like uric acid, dopamine, creatinine, glucose, urea, ascorbic acid NaCl calcium ion, potassium ion vitamin C and vitamin D The quenching efficiency was investigated and analysed by fluorescence spectroscopy when the concentration of all these analytes was monitored with BR added to NCQDS solution as shown in (Fig. 8b), significant variations of the analytes mentioned in were observed of the above. This proves that the developed fluorescence sensing system is highly selective with respect to BR.

Furthermore, the fluorescence quenching mechanism to the BL was investigated by measuring the time course of the fluorescence decay of the fluorophore NCQD in the absence and presence of the quencher BL (100 μM) monitoring the formation of NCQDs at 450 nm in the absence and presence of quencher BL (100 μM) was present. and reliably fit the exponential decay function. The addition of BL reduced the NCQDs’ lifetime from 3.64 ns to 1.61 ns. As shown in (Fig. 9 ), the observed changes in fluorescence decay in NCQDs reflect energy transfer and dynamic quenching due to additional passivation mechanisms, i.e., fluorescence resonance energy transfer (FRET).

Some investigations are conducted to learn more about the sensing process. the emission and absorption spectra of NCQD and BR displayed significant overlap, respectively, indicating that BR could quench NCQD fluorescence through FRET (Fig. 5a). Additionally, the fluorescence decay curves of NCQD exhibit high intensities both in the absence and presence of BR (Fig. 9). Upon the addition of BL, the lifetime of NCQDs decreased from 3.64 ns to 1.61 ns. These alterations in fluorescence decay, as depicted in (Fig. 9), suggest energy transfer and dynamic quenching attributable to a novel deactivation mechanism, namely fluorescence resonance energy transfer (FRET). Moreover, the absorption spectra of BR resemble the emission spectrum of NCQDs, further corroborated by FRET, indicating that the FRET mechanism is accountable for BR sensing (Fig. 5a). These findings refute the hypothesis of NCQD chains and BR forming any ground-state charge transfer complex between the CQDs. It is conceivable that electron transport pathways may also play a role in fluorescence quenching. Furthermore, a theoretical investigation of the sensing mechanism has been conducted to elucidate it using DFT by calculating the energy of frontier molecular orbitals.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Detection of bilirubin on colorimetric test papers
ce:section.ce:para: Fluorescent, colorimetric test papers are used to monitor changes in the intensity and color of the test papers under intense light to determine the presence and content of target analytes Such test these documents have the advantage of being cheap, easy to use, and portable. In this study, fluorescent, colorimetric screening papers based on NCQDs were developed for BR detection. The test plates were dried with 30 μL of 0.001 M NCQDs working solution poured into them. Then, BR solutions of 1.35, 2.68, 4.04, 5.32, 6.62, 9.19, and 10.45 μM were deposited on the prepared colorimetric paper and the changes were observed under UV light (S5). In the absence of BR, pure NCQDs first appeared dark yellow and with varying conc. of BR, the yellow color becomes diminished, which also explains the quenching mechanism [71].

ce:section.ce:section-title: Detection of BR in human blood serum
ce:section.ce:para: A highly sensitive and selective analytical method for detecting BR in human blood, utilizing NCQD (Nitrogen-doped carbon quantum dots), has been developed. Leveraging the exceptional sensitivity and selectivity of the NCQD-based biosensor, we initially employed it for BR detection in human blood samples. To evaluate the accuracy of our approach, a known volume of standard BR was added to clinical serum samples, and its recovery was calculated. The potential of the biosensor array to specifically monitor BR levels in human serum was examined based on our findings. In accordance with a previously published study [72], human blood serum was subjected to centrifugation at 5000 rpm for 15 min on multiple occasions. The resulting supernatant was then diluted 1000 times and spiked with varying concentrations of BR (0, 10, 20, and 30 μM). The recovery of BR using this detection method is within the range of 94.8 % to 104.21 %. as presented in (Figs. 12 , S8). Our approach holds promise for application in primary care, as it covers the BR concentration range from millimolar (cellular) to micromolar (plasma) levels in living organisms. It also holds potential applications in the fields of biology, biomedicine, and biotechnology. Notably, when compared to previously investigated BR sensors the NCQD biosensor demonstrated superior performance for BR detection. The effectiveness of our proposed strategy is underscored by the favorable recoveries observed, making it suitable for practical applications [73].

The portable and user-friendly NCQDs solution-based nano-sensor was also designed using the in-house Android smartphone software. The advantage of smartphone image recognition over naked eye recognition is its accuracy [74].

When the camera takes the picture, the Android app analyzes it with the latest RGB-standard (red, green, space blue) software and displays the results on the smartphone Photographed with the camera as close as possible (6 cm away) to the sample vial without flash. The addition of BL to NCQDS enabled the color change from blue to green to be detected visually. Then, RGB profiling was used to measure color density using a smartphone camera [75]. The value of B decreased steadily, and the value of G increased approximately the BL concentration at 0–56.60 µM, the correlation coefficient is 0.9924 To build a linear relationship using (R + G)/2 as the abscissa, and the BL concentration as the ordinate to superimpose tree again as accuracy of the suggested smartphone detection method [76] We adopted the average value of red and green to reduce the error and improve the accuracy of the results shown in Fig. 13 . Therefore, the smartphone equipped with NCQDs nano-sensor can be used for fast and rapid BL monitoring. All the findings indicated that the proposed smartphone detection method can sensibly detect BL. The calculated R + G/2 value was plotted against the BR concentration, and the resulting graph is plotted in (Fig. 13) with an estimated LOD of 11.74 μM.

In summary, the NCQDs were successfully fabricated using the furnace-assist method, yielding NCQDs with remarkable properties suitable for the selective and instantaneous detection of bilirubin (BR) with a low limit of detection. The NCQDs displayed a characteristic blue fluorescence at 450 nm upon excitation at 360 nm. These synthesized NCQDs demonstrated exceptional stability, water solubility, fluorescence, and biocompatibility. The NCQDs-based nanosensor exhibited outstanding analytical performance, characterized by high selectivity and sensitivity in the linear concentration range of 0–14.20 µM, along with an impressively low limit of detection of 4.86 µM. The interaction of BR with NCQDs resulted in a reduction in quantum dot intensity and lifetime, indicating static quenching through the Förster resonance energy transfer (FRET) mechanism. This FRET mechanism was further validated by overlaying the absorption spectrum of BR with the emission spectrum of NCQDs.The proposed methodology surpasses existing literature methods in terms of its ability to achieve lower detection limits, user-friendliness, portability, photostability, selectivity, and sensitivity for BR detection. Notably, NCQDs exhibited robust stability even in high-salt environments, showcasing resilience against variations in ionic strength within aqueous solutions. Furthermore, NCQDs demonstrated exceptional photostability even under prolonged exposure to UV–Vis light. The observed electron transfer from BR to NCQDs, as indicated by their respective band gaps, underpins the energetic feasibility of such a process. The developed method offers stability, cost-effectiveness, simplicity, and speed, making it well-suited for various applications, including colorimetric test papers. Remarkably, a portable smartphone sensor leveraging NCQDs was also designed and exhibited exceptional recovery and accuracy in real sample BR detection, achieving recovery percentages ranging from 95.1 % to 103.3 %. This underscores the practical potential of the proposed approach in real-world scenarios [77,78].

Himanshu Kumar: Writing – review & editing, Writing – original draft, Software, Project administration, Methodology, Investigation, Formal analysis, Data curation, Conceptualization. Jyoti Duhan: Writing – review & editing, Writing – original draft, Software, Resources. Sangeeta Obrai: Supervision.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: The authors express their sincere gratitude to the Ministry of Human Resource and Development (MHRD), New Delhi, for awarding a research assistantship to Mr. Himanshu Kumar. They also acknowledge Dr. B.R. Ambedkar National Institute of Technology, Jalandhar, for providing the necessary research infrastructure.

J. Alloys Compd.
